Threat.value: Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Negative Appraisals.value: People are not what they seem
I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
I can't rely on other people

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I put on loud music or TV
I dwell on how I used to be before the event

Memory.value: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
I can talk about what happened very easily.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.

vignette:
He is a 46-year-old single man from Oceania who presents with a high burden of post-traumatic stress symptoms following an accident, with a PCL-5 score of 59. He describes living with persistent neck pain, and certain sounds and conversations now have an immediate emotional charge for him. Shouting in Arabic and talk about accidents are especially difficult to hear, and these moments leave him feeling on edge and unsettled. He reports being superalert and watchful much of the time, and his sleep is often broken by trouble falling asleep or waking and struggling to settle again.

His account of the event is fragmented into a few vivid moments, each held in mind like a picture. At the same time, he can talk about what happened very easily, and yet the recollection does not feel distant; it returns to him like a film playing over and over. These memories sit alongside a strong sense that the world is unsafe and people are not what they seem. He says he cannot rely on other people, and he often describes himself as no longer the happy person he used to be, but instead as someone who feels miserable much of the time.

These experiences shape how he gets through the day. When he feels tense or overwhelmed, he puts on loud music or the television to block things out. He also avoids exercise and spends time dwelling on how he used to be before the accident. He has continued to take too many risks or do things that could cause harm, despite the strain this places on him. 

Overall, his symptoms appear to keep one another in place, with the accident-related reminders, the intrusive memory, and his sense of current danger feeding a cycle of vigilance, mistrust, and withdrawal. The cumulative effect is marked distress and reduced confidence in daily life, with a noticeable toll on his sense of safety, mood, and ability to connect with others.
